name: Studio Microphone
description: Classic large-diaphragm studio condenser microphone cradled in a U-shaped shock-mount yoke on a weighted desktop stand, with a coiled cable looping back to the base.
feedback: The 'labels-inside-boolean-ops-are-lost' rule is the single biggest footgun. I wanted to express 'body MINUS portholes-cut-by-grille' compositionally but had to fold every difference INSIDE one label or accept losing both tags. A linter that scans the SCAD source and flags `difference()/intersection() { label(...) ... }` patterns BEFORE compile would have saved 2-3 iterations.

Test loop turnaround is dominated by the openscad-wasm cold start (~5-10s). A persistent watcher mode for the harness (`tsx --watch`) that keeps the engine resident across SCAD edits would convert a 10s edit-test cycle into ~1s. Right now editing the SCAD literal in the test file is the path of least resistance and it's slow.

The harness reports per-label triangle counts but not bounding boxes or centroids. When I built the yoke with `rotate_extrude` + a chop-cube I had to eyeball whether the U actually opened upward — a one-line `bbox` echo per label would have caught one orientation bug instantly without screenshotting.

There's no way from the harness output to know if my labels overlap with each other geometrically (e.g. the cable rings clipping into the base). A `paintByLabel` collision summary ('grille shares 24 tris with body') would help me know when to nudge a region rather than waiting to see colors blotch in the viewer.

The `colors.md` examples lean on simple shapes; a worked example of a label wrapping a multi-step `difference()` (with the 'keep it inside ONE label' rule called out explicitly with an anti-pattern next to it) would have shortened my mental ramp. The text says 'labels are lost' but a side-by-side wrong/right snippet would land it harder.
